title: Kulkuvälineet
description: Learn how to talk about transport in Finnish — add -lla/-llä to say 'by vehicle'
instructions: Practice transport vocabulary and the adessive case (-lla/-llä) used to express means of travel.

questions.text: What is 'bussi' in English?
questions.explanation: 'Bussi' = bus. It's a loanword from English/Swedish, easy to remember!

questions.text: What is the Finnish word for 'train'?
questions.explanation: 'Juna' = train. Other options: ratikka = tram, laiva = ship/boat, metro = metro.

questions.text: What does 'pyörä' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Pyörä' = bicycle (literally 'wheel'). Motorcycle is 'moottoripyörä' (motor + wheel).

questions.text: What is 'lentokone' in English?
questions.explanation: 'Lentokone' = airplane. 'Lento' = flight, 'kone' = machine. Literally 'flying machine'!

questions.text: What does 'laiva' mean?
questions.explanation: 'Laiva' = ship/boat. Common on Finnish-Swedish ferry routes: Helsinki → Stockholm by laiva!

questions.text: What is the Finnish word for 'tram'?
questions.explanation: 'Ratikka' = tram (puhekieli/spoken). The formal word is 'raitiovaunu'. Helsinki has a famous tram network!

questions.text: What case ending is used in Finnish to express 'by means of transport'?
questions.explanation: The adessive case (-lla/-llä) is used to say 'by [transport]'. Example: bussi → bussilla (by bus).

questions.text: How do you say 'by bus' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: bussi + lla = bussilla. The word 'bussi' has back vowels (u, i) so we use -lla (not -llä).

questions.text: How do you say 'by bicycle' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: pyörä + llä = pyörällä. 'Pyörä' contains front vowels (ö, ä) so we use -llä (vowel harmony!).

questions.text: Which vowel harmony rule decides between -lla and -llä?
options: Words with a, o, u → -lla; words with ä, ö, y → -llä
It depends on the first letter of the word
questions.explanation: Vowel harmony: if the word contains back vowels (a, o, u) use -lla. If it contains front vowels (ä, ö, y) use -llä. Example: juna (a) → junalla; pyörä (ä) → pyörällä.

questions.text: Convert to 'by train': juna → ?
questions.explanation: juna + lla = junalla. 'Juna' has back vowel 'a', so we use -lla.

questions.text: Convert to 'by metro': metro → ?
questions.explanation: metro + lla = metrolla. 'Metro' has back vowels (e and o), so we use -lla.

questions.text: Convert to 'by airplane': lentokone → ?
questions.explanation: lentokone + ella = lentokoneella. Words ending in 'e' double the vowel before the case ending: lentokone → lentokone + e + lla = lentokoneella.

questions.text: Convert to 'by taxi': taksi → ?
questions.explanation: taksi + lla = taksilla. 'Taksi' has back vowels, so -lla.

questions.text: How do you say 'I go to university by bus'?
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen bussilla yliopistolle.' The adessive -lla on bussi (bussilla) means 'by bus'. Note: yliopistolle (allative) = to the university.

questions.text: How do you say 'I go by ship from Turku to Stockholm'?
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen laivalla Turusta Tukholmaan.' Laivalla (by ship), Turusta (from Turku, elative -sta), Tukholmaan (to Stockholm, illative -an).

questions.text: Translate: 'I go by bike to work.' (Mä meen ___ töihin)
questions.explanation: pyörä + llä = pyörällä (by bike). 'Töihin' = to work (allative plural of työ).

questions.text: Complete: 'Mä meen ___ Helsinkiin.' (I go by train to Helsinki)
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen junalla Helsinkiin.' Junalla = by train (juna + lla). Helsinkiin = to Helsinki (illative).

questions.text: Complete: 'Mä meen ___ Saksaan.' (I go by airplane to Germany)
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen lentokoneella Saksaan.' Lentokoneella = by airplane. Saksaan = to Germany.

questions.text: How do you say 'on foot' in Finnish? (This is an exception!)
questions.explanation: 'Kävellen' = on foot. This is an exception — it does NOT use -lla/-llä. 'Kävellä' is the verb 'to walk', and kävellen is its instructive form.

questions.text: Why doesn't 'on foot' use -lla/-llä like other transport words?
options: 'Kävellen' comes from a verb (kävellä = to walk), not a noun transport word
Finnish has no word for walking
questions.explanation: All other transport words are nouns (bussi, juna, pyörä), so they get -lla/-llä. But 'on foot' uses the verb 'kävellä' (to walk) in its instructive form: kävellen. It has a different grammatical origin.

questions.text: Complete: 'Mä meen ___ kauppaan.' (I go on foot to the store)
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen kävellen kauppaan.' = I go on foot to the store. Remember: kävellen, not jalalla!

questions.text: How do you say 'by motorcycle'?
questions.explanation: moottoripyörä + llä = moottoripyörällä. The word contains front vowels (ö, ä) so -llä is used.

questions.text: What is the Finnish word for 'metro/subway'?
questions.explanation: 'Metro' is the same word in Finnish! It's a loanword used in Helsinki's public transport.

questions.text: How do you say 'I go by metro from Pasila to the city centre'?
questions.explanation: 'Mä meen metrolla Pasilasta keskustaan.' Metrolla = by metro. Pasilasta = from Pasila (elative -sta). Keskustaan = to the centre (illative -an).
